mnemonics.title: Euler's Formula
mnemonics.content: V ERry E xcellent R uby's P iano: Vertices - Edges + Regions = 2 (V- E + R = 2)

mnemonics.title: Max Edges in Planar
mnemonics.content: 3n - 6: Think '3 Times n minus 6, Planar limit for you!' (for n ≥ 3)

mnemonics.title: Non-Planar Graphs
mnemonics.content: K5 and K3,3 - Just Remember: 'Keep 5 and 3-3 Away from Planar!'

mnemonics.title: Four Color Theorem
mnemonics.content: 4 Colors: Like 4 corners of a map - enough to color any country map without conflicts!

acronyms.usage: Used to test planarity - contains 5 vertices with all possible edges

acronyms.expansion: Complete bipartite graph with two sets of 3 vertices each (non-planar)
acronyms.usage: Three utilities problem - cannot connect 3 houses to 3 utilities without crossing
v - e + r = 2 for connected planar graphs

acronyms.expansion: Planar if no K5 or K3,3 subdivision exists
acronyms.usage: Test for non-planarity of any graph

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pizza (regions) cut into slices (faces). Count vertices as people, edges as cuts, regions as pizza pieces. v - e + r always equals 2!
Draw 5 points. Connect every pair - you'll always need at least one crossing because 10 > 3(5)-6 = 9
Three houses and three utilities - try to connect each house to each utility without lines crossing - impossible!
Each edge is shared by 2 faces like a wall between two rooms - counting walls from both sides gives 2e
A triangulated graph is like a triangular mesh - every triangle closed, no more edges can be added!

analogies.analogy: Like drawing a subway map on a flat surface - all stations (vertices) and tracks (edges) without any track crossing over another.
Like countries on a map - each country is a face bounded by borders (edges), and the whole world map is the outer face.
Like a map of countries and their borders - each country (face) becomes a dot, borders become lines connecting dots!
Like different shaped balloons all derived from the same base ball by stretching or adding wrinkles - same fundamental shape!
A bridge across a river divides land into two separate pieces - similarly, a bridge edge separates two faces.
